description: This is the vendored, unmodified Bootstrap v3.1.1 minified bundle; the regexes used (e.g. /.*(?=#[^\s]*$)/, /#[A-Za-z]/, /(38|40|27)/, /\s?auto?\s?/i) are simple, non-nested-quantifier patterns with no catastrophic backtracking potential, and the flagged line is really the entire minified file being matched as one line rather than an actual vulnerable regex construct.

This is unmodified, minified Bootstrap v3.1.1 library code (a well-known third-party file, not custom application logic). The uses of a.extend (jQuery.extend) here are shallow merges of static plugin default option objects, not recursive merges of untrusted/attacker-controlled JSON keys, so there's no reachable prototype pollution sink shown in this snippet.

This just serializes visible form fields from the page's own DOM into a plain object for JSON.stringify; field.name is not attacker-controlled JSON merged into a target object, and even if a field named '__proto__' existed, assigning a string value via bracket notation is a no-op for the prototype setter, so no real pollution occurs.

The regex pattern is a fixed literal string with no nested/overlapping quantifiers, so it has linear-time matching and no catastrophic backtracking potential; document.cookie length is also browser-bounded, giving no realistic ReDoS vector.

This is a single non-recursive bracket assignment (not a merge/clone pattern), and curHelp.helpElement is populated internally from lesson hint data rather than raw untrusted user input; even if a key of "__proto__" reached this line, a plain assignment merely reassigns that instance's own prototype rather than polluting Object.prototype globally, so the classic prototype-pollution exploit chain doesn't apply here.

The flagged line is the tail of the internal cleanData loop (`u[o]&&(delete u[o],...)`) and contains no regular expression construction or usage at all — this is a vendored, minified third-party jQuery 1.10.2 file where the line-number attribution appears to be off, and even the file's actual regexes (Sizzle selector patterns) are not on this line or exercised here in a way that constitutes a genuine ReDoS defect in application code.

The flagged line 6 contains jQuery's cleanData/wrapAll/css/ajax/animation internals with no recursive merge or attacker-controlled key assignment; the real deep-merge sink (jQuery.extend, CVE-2019-11358) lives earlier in the minified blob, so this appears to be a mislocated generic keyword match rather than an actual prototype-pollution sink at the cited location.

This is a vendored, minified jQuery UI 1.10.4 library file; the flagged line is an artifact of line-splitting in a single-line minified blob and doesn't correspond to any single identifiable regex with catastrophic backtracking on attacker-controlled input. The regexes actually present in this file (e.g., escapeRegex, keyCode matchers, CSS property matchers) operate on short, largely developer-controlled strings and have no known ReDoS CVE for this version.

This is vendored, minified third-party jQuery UI 1.10.4 library code; the flagged line is a year-range parsing helper for the datepicker widget with no prototype/object property assignment resembling __proto__, constructor.prototype, or similar pollution sinks. The generic rule signature appears to have misfired on minified variable names rather than an actual prototype pollution primitive.

The regex /^([^#]+)/ uses a single negated character class with no nested or overlapping quantifiers, so it runs in linear time and cannot cause catastrophic backtracking; this is a benign URL-hash-stripping pattern, not a ReDoS vector.

The regex has three adjacent quantifiers (\s*, .*?, \s*) with overlapping character classes, giving polynomial (not exponential) worst-case backtracking, and it runs inside vendored Ace editor code only when a user explicitly invokes the 'align text' command on their own selected editor lines — not on untrusted/remote input, so real-world exploitability as a DoS vector is minimal.

This is minified Backbone.js v1.4.0 library code (identifiable by version string and structure) with no `__proto__`, `constructor.prototype`, or dynamic key merge into object prototypes; the tool's generic '__proto_pollution__' tag appears to be a mis-detection on unrelated library code with no actual prototype pollution sink.

The regex /.*(?=#[^\s]*$)/ has no nested quantifiers causing exponential backtracking — at worst it's O(n^2) from linear backtracking of .* combined with a linear lookahead scan, not catastrophic ReDoS. This is also vendored, minified Bootstrap tab.js library code operating on bounded href attribute strings, not attacker-controlled unbounded input.

This is minified Bootstrap tooltip/popover code copying whitelisted option keys from getDefaults() to a fresh local object literal `b = {}`, not merging into Object.prototype or a shared prototype chain; `a` here is a string key from `a.each` iteration over `this._options`, not user-controlled `__proto__` traversal. No sink writes to a prototype object, so this is a mis-classification of standard jQuery/Bootstrap option-merging code.

Line 4 is part of jQuery's attr getter/setter logic and contains no regex construction at all — the actual regex literals in this minified library are defined earlier (lines 1-2, e.g. Sizzle selector regexes), so the finding is mislocated/misattributed. This is also vendored third-party jQuery library code, not application logic, so even if a regex elsewhere had backtracking risk, there's no evidence here that untrusted input reaches it at this reported location.

This is minified jQuery 2.1.4's own internal source (n.extend/$.extend implementation), a well-known third-party library file, not application code; the pattern match on generic object-merge logic is a mis-classification rather than an exploitable prototype pollution flaw in WebGoat's own code.

The regex /^([\-+])=\s*(\d+\.?\d*)/ has no nested or overlapping quantifiers and is anchored, so it matches in linear time with no catastrophic backtracking potential; this is a mischaracterization of a simple, safe pattern in vendored jQuery UI code.

The key `this.id` comes from `parent.uniqueId()`, which reads or generates a DOM element's `id` attribute during ancestor traversal — it is not attacker-controlled JSON/object input flowing into a generic merge/clone, so it cannot be set to `__proto__` via untrusted data in the way CWE-1321 requires. This is bundled third-party jQuery UI library code with no known prototype-pollution CVE at this line; the scanner is flagging generic bracket-notation assignment without exploitable taint.

This is the unmodified, minified jQuery UI v1.12.1 vendor library file; the tool merely flagged some regex literal within it without demonstrating any attacker-controlled input reaching a vulnerable pattern, and this is well-known, widely-audited third-party library code rather than an application-specific ReDoS defect.

This is the unmodified, minified jQuery UI 1.12.1 library file bundled as a static vendor asset; the flagged line is generic widget/plugin code (t.widget.extend, jQuery.extend usage) with no attacker-controlled merge into object prototypes, and the tool's regex-based prototype-pollution matcher is misfiring on common jQuery patterns in third-party library code.

The regex /^([^#]+)/ is a single negated-character-class match with no nested or overlapping quantifiers, so it has no ambiguous matching paths and runs in linear time — it cannot cause catastrophic backtracking regardless of input length.

This is the entire minified jQuery 3.4.1 vendor library collapsed onto one line; the tool gives no specific regex construct or reachable untrusted-input path, and jQuery's regexes here are well-audited internal parsing/selector engine patterns, not user-supplied ReDoS-prone expressions. Flagging a whole third-party library line as '__redos__' without isolating an actual exponential-backtracking pattern indicates a mis-classification rather than a genuine exploitable defect in this codebase.
Insecure function usage: __redos__ (regex)

The flagged line 17 is merely the UMD module-wrapper boilerplate for the third-party Polyglot.js library (Airbnb), not a prototype-pollution sink; the library's only merge logic (`extend`) uses `hasOwnProperty` checks on the source object and there is no `__proto__`/`constructor.prototype` manipulation reachable from untrusted input in the shown code.

This is the entire minified, unmodified third-party RequireJS 2.3.6 library bundled as a static vendor asset; the flagged 'insecure function' is just the library's generic mixin/config-merge routine, with no visible evidence that attacker-controlled data reaches it as untrusted input. The finding reflects a naive pattern match on vendor code rather than a demonstrated exploitable path.

The regex is linear: a single optional (\w+): prefix followed by disjoint literal and negated-character-class groups, with no nested or overlapping quantifiers that could cause exponential backtracking, so it is not vulnerable to ReDoS.

This is RequireJS's text.js plugin; buildMap[name]=content only executes when masterConfig.isBuild is true (i.e., during the offline r.js build/optimizer step), and `name` is an AMD module/resource identifier from source code, not untrusted runtime user input. The bracket assignment is a benign build-time cache keyed by module path, not a reachable prototype-pollution sink from web-facing input.

This is the standard minified Underscore.js 1.10.2 library bundled as a static vendor asset (webgoat UI resource); the flagged line is generic global-object detection boilerplate (self/global/Function('return this')()) with no dynamic property assignment from untrusted keys, so the prototype-pollution rule mismatched on library setup code rather than actual pollution-prone merge/extend logic.

This is third-party minified Modernizr library code; the regexes used (e.g. simple char-class replacements like /([a-z])-([a-z])/g, /^-/, /([A-Z])/g) are linear-time patterns with no nested quantifiers or catastrophic backtracking potential, and none operate on attacker-controlled input at runtime—they process fixed CSS/DOM strings.

This is the third-party Modernizr 3.6.0 minified library performing standard feature-detection property assignment on its own internal Modernizr object, not a user-controlled recursive merge/clone of attacker-supplied keys; there's no untrusted input reaching object key assignment in a way that enables prototype pollution.

This is a plain array index assignment (`this.percentage[this.dragged] = percentage`) where `this.dragged` is an internally-set 0/1 flag, not a user-controlled property key like `__proto__` or `constructor.prototype`; there is no dynamic key derived from untrusted input, so this is a mis-classification rather than real prototype pollution.

toolbarOpts.customTemplates is a caller-supplied config object (bundled third-party wysihtml5 library, not remote/untrusted input), and the single-level `tpl[t] = ...` assignment merely reassigns tpl's own prototype/property rather than performing the recursive merge pattern needed to pollute Object.prototype globally; no attacker-controlled network path reaches this constructor argument in the shown code.

The regex /((https?:\/\/|www\.)[^\s<]{3,})/gi uses a single bounded quantifier on a negated character class with no nested/overlapping repetition, so it matches in linear time and has no catastrophic backtracking potential.

This is a vendored third-party minified library (nanoScrollerJS) where the only regexes (`/msie 7./i`, `/Firefox\/\d{2}\./`, `/\D+/g`, a lookahead check) are simple, non-backtracking patterns applied to the browser's own navigator.userAgent/appVersion strings, not attacker-controlled network input, and none exhibit exponential/catastrophic backtracking structure.

This builds a plain local object from serialized form fields; even if an attacker sets a field name to '__proto__', the value assigned is always a string (from serializeArray), and the __proto__ setter silently ignores non-object assignments, so no prototype pollution occurs. There is also no merge into a shared/global object or Object.prototype here.

This is a JUnit test file using hardcoded literals as mock method arguments to test password-handling logic, not a real credential; CWE-259 (hardcoded password) does not apply to test fixtures exercising a mocked repository.

This is a misclassification: the flagged line generates an RSA-2048 keypair (KeyPairGenerator), not a hash function, so CWE-328 (weak hash) doesn't apply; it's also test-only code (JWTHeaderJKUEndpointTest) used to set up a mock key for a WebGoat lesson, not production cryptography reachable from untrusted input.

This is a JUnit test file using a hardcoded literal password purely to simulate a login request against a local mock MVC test server; it is test code with no production credential storage or authentication logic, so CWE-259 (hardcoded password) does not meaningfully apply.

This is a JUnit test file exercising the WebGoat InsecureDeserialization lesson; the hardcoded base64 token is a fixture payload used to test the lesson's version-check logic, not a real credential/secret in production code.

The flagged line generates an RSA-2048 key pair via KeyPairGenerator, which is asymmetric key generation, not a hash function — CWE-328 (weak hash) is a mis-classification here. Additionally this is WebGoat test/integration code intentionally simulating a JWT attack scenario for a security-training lesson, not production logic handling untrusted input.
This is a hardcoded test credential used only in a Playwright UI test to register a throwaway account during automated testing, not a production authentication secret.

destinationFile is built by resolving the unsanitized multipartFile.getOriginalFilename() against destinationDir with no normalization/containment check, so a filename like ../../../etc/passwd lets Files.copy write outside the intended per-user directory.

This is WebGoat's intentionally-vulnerable JKU lesson: the attacker-controlled JWT's 'jku' header claim is used unchecked to build the JwkProviderBuilder URL and fetch the verification key, letting an attacker host their own JWKS and forge a validly-signed token; no allowlist/host validation is present in the shown code.

The flagged line only decodes a JWT token (JWT.decode); there is no SQL query construction or database interaction anywhere in the shown code, so a SQL injection classification is a mistagging of this line.
This line instantiates an RSA KeyPairGenerator, not a hash function, so CWE-328 (weak hash algorithm) does not apply; the RSA key generation itself uses 2048-bit keys, which is not inherently weak.

The value stored in the session (basicAuth) is derived from a server-side authenticated principal name and a hard-coded secrets array, not from unvalidated external input, so there's no actual mixing of trusted/untrusted data; this is WebGoat's own intentionally-simplistic lesson code, not a genuine trust boundary violation.

The code explicitly uses MD5 (a cryptographically broken hash algorithm) via MessageDigest.getInstance("MD5") to hash a secret value, matching CWE-328; note this is WebGoat, an intentionally vulnerable training app where this weak-hash usage is the deliberate lesson content rather than an accidental defect.

The session attributes store a server-generated random secret and its MD5 hash, not attacker-controlled or untrusted data, so no trust boundary is actually violated; this is WebGoat's intentionally insecure teaching lesson using session storage for a hint mechanism, not a genuine CWE-501 defect.

This line just stores a server-generated key pair into the HttpSession for later use in the same lesson flow; no untrusted input is crossing a trust boundary here, so CWE-501 (Trust Boundary Violation) does not fit — it's ordinary session-state usage in a WebGoat teaching exercise, not a real trust-boundary defect.

java.util.Random is not cryptographically secure and is used to scramble a password reset token; for the admin user it is seeded with key.length(), a tiny guessable value, making the reset link predictable/brute-forceable.

Both username_login and password_login are untrusted request parameters concatenated directly into the SQL string; despite using a PreparedStatement object, no parameter placeholders (?) are used, so the query is built via string concatenation and is directly injectable.

The `username` parameter comes directly from an HTTP request param and is concatenated into a SQL string executed via Statement.executeQuery, with no sanitization or parameterization — classic SQL injection, and notably the code even uses PreparedStatement correctly just below for the INSERT, highlighting the inconsistency.

The password variable is initialized to the hardcoded literal "dave" and used as a fallback/default credential value compared against user input for authentication, which is a textbook hardcoded-password pattern regardless of the subsequent DB lookup attempt.

User-controlled `action` is concatenated directly into a SQL string and executed via Statement.executeQuery() with no sanitization or parameterization, allowing classic SQL injection; this is WebGoat's intentionally vulnerable SQL injection lesson.

User-supplied `query` parameter from the HTTP request is passed directly into `statement.executeUpdate(query)` with no sanitization or parameterization, allowing arbitrary SQL execution; this is WebGoat's intentionally vulnerable SQL injection lesson demonstrating exactly this flaw.
User-controlled `name` and `auth_tan` are concatenated directly into a SQL string with no sanitization or parameterization and executed via Statement.executeQuery, a classic SQL injection sink.

The flagged line merely instantiates a Calendar object; there is no hashing algorithm used anywhere in this snippet, so a weak-hash (CWE-328) classification is a mis-classification. The actual defect nearby is SQL injection via string concatenation (line 138), not a hashing weakness.

The query string is built via direct concatenation of user-controlled auth_tan (and other fields) into SQL text, then executed with statement.execute() without parameterization or sanitization, which is a textbook SQL injection pattern; this is WebGoat's intentionally vulnerable lesson code demonstrating exactly this flaw.

The `accountName` (userid) parameter is concatenated directly into the SQL string and passed to prepareStatement, so the ? placeholder only covers login_count while userid remains unparameterized and attacker-controlled, allowing classic SQL injection.

The `query` parameter comes directly from user-controlled @RequestParam input and is passed unmodified into Statement.executeQuery(query), with no parameterization or sanitization, making it a direct, exploitable SQL injection sink.

The `query` parameter comes directly from an HTTP request and is passed unmodified into `Statement.executeUpdate()` with no parameterization, escaping, or validation, allowing arbitrary SQL injection.

The `column` request parameter is concatenated directly into the SQL query string used to build the ORDER BY clause, with no whitelist/validation shown before it reaches `prepareStatement`; PreparedStatement placeholders can't parametrize identifiers like column names, so this concatenation is exploitable for SQL injection via the `column` query parameter.

The zip entry name e.getName() is used directly to construct the extraction path via `new File(tmpZipDirectory.toFile(), e.getName())` with no validation against '../' sequences, then written via Files.copy — a classic Zip Slip that lets a malicious zip write files outside tmpZipDirectory.

The Cookie objects for "access_token" (both here and at line 120-121 where the actual JWT is set) never call setHttpOnly(true) or setSecure(true), so the auth cookie is readable/modifiable via client-side JavaScript and sent over plaintext HTTP. Even though this specific branch clears the cookie with an empty value, it reflects the same unprotected cookie-construction pattern used to carry the actual JWT elsewhere in the same handler.

The cookie carrying the JWT access_token (and its cleared version on line 125-126) is constructed and added to the response without any setSecure(true) or setHttpOnly(true) call visible in the shown code, so the auth cookie is transmitted without the Secure flag and is vulnerable to disclosure over unencrypted channels.

The cookie created at line 74-76 sets Path and Secure but never calls setHttpOnly(true), so the session/auth cookie added via response.addCookie() at line 77 is exposed to client-side script access (missing HttpOnly), matching CWE-1004.

This cookie is being cleared (empty value, maxAge=0) as part of a WebGoat lesson cleanup endpoint, not used to transmit session/sensitive data, so missing Secure/HttpOnly flags here have no real security impact; WebGoat is also an intentionally vulnerable training app where this endpoint's purpose is just to reset lesson state.

The cookie sets Secure but never calls setHttpOnly(true), so it is readable via JavaScript/XSS; since this stores a session-hijack-relevant value, missing HttpOnly is a genuine instance of CWE-1004 regardless of WebGoat's intentionally-vulnerable teaching context.

fromString() calls ObjectInputStream.readObject() on attacker-controllable Base64 data with no type filtering or validation, which is classic unsafe Java deserialization (this is WebGoat's deserialization lesson, purpose-built to demonstrate this exact CWE-502 flaw).
User-supplied request parameter is base64-decoded and passed directly to ObjectInputStream.readObject() with no class allowlist or filtering, a textbook unsafe deserialization of untrusted input.

The flagged line only configures request authorization (authorizeHttpRequests/permitAll for specific paths); it does not disable Spring Security's CSRF protection, which remains enabled by default unless a separate .csrf(...) call disables it elsewhere (not shown here). PermitAll on a POST endpoint's authorization does not itself create a CSRF vulnerability.

The reported line and surrounding code only configure authorizeHttpRequests matchers (permitAll for static resources); no csrf() disable or CSRF-related logic is visible at or near this location, so the CWE-352 classification appears mis-attributed to this line.
A password/secret value is hardcoded directly in source code as a public static final String, which is a textbook CWE-259 instance regardless of this being a WebGoat teaching lesson.

ADMIN_PASSWORD_LINK is a hardcoded secret/token value embedded directly in source code and used as part of an admin password-reset link, matching the CWE-259 hardcoded-credential pattern regardless of the surrounding lesson context.

PINCODE is generated with java.util.Random, a non-cryptographic PRNG with a predictable seed/state, and is stored as a static final secret value used presumably for access/verification in this WebGoat challenge — this matches CWE-330 as the value can be predicted rather than relying on a CSPRNG like SecureRandom.

This is WebGoat, a deliberately vulnerable teaching application; LEAKED_TOKEN is a fake placeholder string embedded in a simulated stack-trace/log output used to teach the verbose-error-leak lesson, not a real credential used for authentication or access control anywhere in the code.

This is WebGoat lesson code (securitymisconfiguration package) intentionally simulating a leaked API key returned by a mock actuator endpoint for teaching purposes; the constant is not a real credential used for authentication anywhere in the application.

This is WebGoat, an intentionally vulnerable training application, and this class is explicitly a lesson demonstrating exploitation of default credentials — the hard-coded admin/admin values are pedagogical fixtures, not a defect in production code.

This is WebGoat's intentionally vulnerable lesson code (variable literally named DeliberatelyInsecure) used to teach access-control flaws, not a real credential securing production access; the hardcoded salt is pedagogical, not exploitable in a real system.

User-controlled input (account, operator, injection request params) is concatenated directly into a SQL string and executed via Statement.executeQuery with no sanitization or parameterization, a classic SQL injection sink; this is WebGoat's intentionally vulnerable lesson demonstrating exactly this flaw.

This is WebGoat lesson/demo code — the constant is a simulated password for a fictional in-lesson user ('Tom'), not a credential protecting any real resource or authentication path, matching the test/mock-code exclusion rather than a genuine hardcoded-password defect.
